In addition to the above stats from XLE’s top five stocks, here are the whole portfolio’s P/E ratio, price/book ratio, and forward earnings growth rate courtesy of State Street:

  • P/E: 19.8.

  • Price/book: 2.3.

  • Price/cash flow: 9.45.

  • EPS growth: 6%.

And here are some similar metrics for the S&P 500:

  • P/E: 23.6.

  • Price/book: 5.15.

  • Price/cash flow: 19.

  • EPS growth: 14%.

As you can see, XLE trades at lower multiples than the broader U.S. markets. However, it also has much less growth. Whereas the biggest XLE stocks saw their earnings decline last year and are expected to see them rise only 6% going forward, the S&P 500 has been growing its earnings and is expected to keep growing them at 14% CAGR. Ultimately, this means that the SPY has a lower PEG ratio than XLE has. However, there are serious concerns about whether the big AI companies (which make up the bulk of the S&P 500’s top 10) can actually hit their earnings forecasts long term, when they are projected to spend $700 billion on AI-related CAPEX in 2026. Their free cash flow is already declining, and their earnings will start getting hit by heavy depreciation in the next 12 months. Given that this is the case, I think that the SPY components will probably grow their earnings less than analysts are expecting in the year ahead and that XLE will prove to be a comparably good value in the period.

Other Fund Characteristics

In addition to trading at comparatively low multiples, XLE has some other attractive characteristics that are worth mentioning. These include:

  • A 2.7% portfolio dividend yield, more than double that of the S&P 500.

  • 22 stocks, a reasonable amount of diversification for a sector fund.

  • A very low 0.08% management fee.

  • A narrow 0.02% bid-ask spread.

Broadly, these characteristics suggest that XLE is inexpensive (in the sense of not paying too much of your money to fund managers and market makers), diversified, and with above-average near-term income potential.

One Risk to Keep in Mind

Despite the fact that I am overall bullish on XLE, I do recognize one very serious risk factor for the fund:

Intermittent weakness in oil prices.

I think oil prices will hold up reasonably well over the very long term. However, they can be quite volatile, and it’s not at all uncommon for them to be below many companies' breakeven for years. A good example was the COVID-19 lockdown period of 2020-early 2021, when many XLE components were unprofitable for the better part of a year.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on XLE is that it is a long-unloved fund whose time has come. Owning it is a great way to get some oil & gas exposure, as well as some passive income, into your portfolio. With oil prices being surprisingly resilient and generative AI providing a new demand catalyst, you’ll be wanting oil & gas exposure. Owing to its 0.08% management fee, adequate diversification, and a narrow bid-ask spread, XLE might just be the place to get it.

data.title: Take Two Interactive Software: Strong Bookings Prospects Amid Selloff (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the past few months, all manner of software stocks have been hammered, as suddenly investors are no longer enticed by recurring revenue streams and the high margins that these businesses provide, preferring instead to bet their chips on highly in-demand semiconductor stocks that are directly feeding the AI data center boom.

I caution investors not to follow the crowd too closely, especially as the recent selloff has positioned many previously expensive stocks at much more reasonable prices. Even gaming companies, such as Take Two Software (TTWO), have been heavily impacted, and the stock is down over 20% from December peaks above $260.

I last wrote a "Sell" article on Take Two in January, when the stock was trading at $250. At the time, I had argued that the stock commanded too rich a valuation premium, especially in the wake of a highly competitive gaming industry with platforms like Roblox (RBLX) gaining substantially in popularity. While I don’t necessarily think Take Two is a buy just yet, the recent drawdown in its valuation makes it a much more interesting play, especially amid a recent burst in bookings that drove a bump to full-year bookings expectations. We’re also aware of Take Two’s very strong pipeline ahead for fiscal FY27, which makes me more confident in upping my rating on the stock to "Neutral."

In my view, at current share prices, I see a more balanced bull and bear case for Take Two. On the bright side for the company:

  • Diverse portfolio of games to span interests and gaming levels. Take Two has an incredibly broad range of titles, spanning from casual racing and puzzle apps built for mobile, to sporting titles (NBA2k26), and the genre-bending Grand Theft Auto franchise, which will get its latest installment in late 2026.
  • Recurring and license sales fuel consistent bookings growth. Unlike traditional gaming companies, which primarily earn one-time revenue from game sales at launch, Take Two’s portfolio of apps and selection of in-game passes and purchases has also created a healthy recurring revenue stream that is also growing at a double-digit y/y pace.

At the same time, however, we are aware of a number of risks:

  • Casual gaming has no shortage of competitors. Some of the most popular games in 2025 were designed not by professional game studios, but simply by users and enthusiasts on the Roblox platform. AI is also leveling the playing field, enabling creatives and designers without technical chops to also create their own games at a fraction of the cost of a studio.
  • Reliance on M&A to achieve growth. Historically, Take Two has leveraged its balance sheet to gain market share in the >$200 billion annual gaming market, as evidenced by the company’s splashy acquisition of Zynga. It has continually snapped up smaller game studios every year, recently spending $460 million to buy a studio called Gearbox (home of the Borderlands franchise). The company is now in a slight net debt position, and investors may eventually sour on the company’s “buy rather than build” approach.

What ultimately keeps me on the sidelines for Take Two, however, is that despite the recent valuation meltdown, Take Two remains decently expensive. At current share prices near $200, Take Two trades at a market cap of $37.76 billion. Netting off the $2.36 billion of cash and $3.07 billion of debt on Take Two’s most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $38.47 billion.

For the remainder of FY26, Take Two has boosted its outlook to $6.65-$6.70 billion, a 3% bump from $6.40-$6.50 billion previously. It’s also now expecting meaningfully higher adjusted EBITDA at $657-$681 million ($669 million midpoint, or a 10.0% margin on bookings and 10% higher than previously).

For next year FY27 (the year ending in March 2027), Wall Street is currently expecting Take Two to deliver $9.18 billion in bookings, or 37% y/y growth. As a reminder, the main driver here is the expected November contribution from Grand Theft Auto VI, a franchise that has not had a mainline update in years. If we assume a similar 10% margin next year, adjusted EBITDA in FY27 would be $918 million. This positions Take Two’s valuation multiples at:

  • 42x EV/FY27 adjusted EBITDA
  • 57x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

To this end, I do think strength is already priced into this stock and caps further upside. For now, stay on the sidelines here and invest elsewhere.

Q3 download

That said, we will acknowledge that even before Grand Theft Auto VI comes into the picture, Take Two’s existing slate is already driving tremendous performance for the company. Take a look at the company’s fiscal Q3 (December quarter) results below:

Take Two’s revenue grew 25% y/y to $1.70 billion, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $1.58 billion (+16% y/y) by a wide nine-point margin. Meanwhile, bookings grew at an even faster 28% y/y pace to $1.76 billion. As a reminder, when it comes to games that are supported by in-app revenue and not upfront license sales, consistent with other gaming companies, Take Two recognizes bookings first (when customers actually make a purchase). Revenue is then recognized as time passes or as items are used up. As such, strong bookings are a good indicator of the company’s strong future revenue trajectory.

The chart below gives good orientation as to Take Two’s recently released and upcoming slate. Recent anchor releases in the past few months include NBA2K26, as well as new Red Dead Redemption and Borderlands releases:

Online, recurring spending from the company's NBA2K26 and Grand Theft Auto online titles drove the bookings success for the quarter. Per CEO Strauss Zelnick's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

NBA 2K26 delivered another stellar quarter, yielding significant upside to our forecast. To date, the title has sold-in approximately 8 million units, representing a high single-digit percentage increase over NBA 2K25. Recurrent consumer spending, daily active users and MyCAREER daily active users all grew 30% year-over-year. Based on its phenomenal year-to-date performance, NBA 2K is on track to generate the highest level of annual net bookings and recurrent consumer spending in franchise history [...]

The Grand Theft Auto series also vastly outpaced our forecast with recurrent consumer spending growth of 27%, led by GTA Online's A Safehouse in the Hills update, which featured long-awaited Mansion properties and the return of the fan-favorite protagonist, Michael De Santa. Full game sales of Grand Theft Auto V remains strong with the title now having sold in over 225 million units since its launch in 2013. GTA+ continues to thrive with membership levels nearly doubling over the same period last year, and we're excited about its potential to add even more value to the player experience in the future.

Though it hasn't issued formal guidance yet for FY27, the company is projecting Grand Theft Auto VI will drive record net bookings next year.

Already, even ahead of that launch, Take Two's adjusted EBITDA has roughly doubled, up 97% y/y to $174.8 million (or a 10% margin against bookings).

Key takeaways

Take Two Interactive Software has taken a sharp nosedive over the past few months, and while I acknowledge that the stock remains quite expensive and has a valuation premium to burn off, none of the downside is due to fundamentals. We're encouraged by the company's strong bookings ahead of the highly anticipated release of Take Two's most important franchise, Grand Theft Auto. Add this stock to your watchlist, but don't buy just yet.

data.title: Adobe: The Best Time To Buy Is Often When It Is The Most Difficult
data.description:
In The Midst Of A Historic Drawdown

After researching ServiceNow (NOW) and Trade Desk (TTD) over the last few days, I continue my study of the software industry with Adobe (ADBE), a pioneer in the software-as-a-service (SaaS) business model. Even though it was a highly controversial decision at the time, Adobe was among the first companies to transition from selling perpetual software in a boxed format to offering a cloud-based solution called Adobe Creative Cloud. ADBE unveiled its Creative Cloud subscription service back in April 2012, and it is only now, almost 14 years later, that this model is starting to be questioned by the investing community. If we look into the rear-view mirror, there is no question that Adobe has been operating a world-class SaaS business, but financial markets are forward-looking in nature, and that's where the troubles start.

Adobe's share price reached an all-time high of $699.54 in November 2021, meaning that today's low of ~$255.00 represent a drawdown of more than 60%, which is one of the largest in the company's history. To find a more severe drawdown on Adobe's stock, we have to go back to the global financial crisis of 2008 or to the dot-com bubble that peaked in March 2000. This is how pessimistic market participants have become about the company's growth prospects.

In fact, Adobe is now trading at prices last seen during the depths of the COVID-19 crash, and that is despite much higher revenues and free cash flows. It is also worth noting that in the meantime, the number of diluted shares outstanding went from 485 million at the end of 2020 to 427 million at the end of 2025, a decline of ~12% inclusive of the impacts associated with the large share-based compensation. However, this time around, the worries are not about a major market meltdown triggered by a global pandemic, but about the impacts of artificial intelligence on traditional cloud-based offerings like those offered by Adobe.

As discussed in a previous article, Adobe is far from the only one caught in the middle of a historic drawdown, as it is also the case for pretty much the entire SaaS industry, including names like ServiceNow (NOW), Salesforce (CRM), Intuit (INTU), Workday (WDAY) or Atlassian (TEAM), among many others. The selling has been indiscriminate so far, and I think this is where the opportunity lies. What if high-quality companies like Adobe and ServiceNow can not only adapt, but actually benefit from the rise of artificial intelligence? What if ADBE's offering eventually acts as a platform for AI deployment, instead of being displaced by it?

After reaching an all-time high of $239.62 in January 2025, ServiceNow's share price is currently hovering around $104.00, a drawdown of almost 60%. As illustrated in the chart below, this is the largest decline since the company's initial public offering in June 2012.

Everyone will agree that it has never been easier to create text, images and videos online, as one can simply use artificial intelligence tools like OpenAI's ChatGPT or Anthropic's Claude. It has also become increasingly difficult to distinguish between authentic human content and artificially-generated content. However, equating those facts with the death of Adobe's product suite is quite a stretch, especially knowing how tools like Photoshop, Illustrator or Premiere Pro are deeply embedded in many workflows. There are significant switching costs at play here, starting with compatibility issues as well as the technical knowledge required to be productive using those complex tools. I do not want to minimize the challenges facing Adobe in a world of rapidly growing AI usage, but at the end of the day, I expect Adobe's customer base to be a lot more sticky than what is currently priced by the market right now.

Double-Digit Revenue Growth

For the full-year of 2025, Adobe reported total revenues of $23.8 billion, an increase of 10.5% versus the $21.5 billion reported in the prior year. Looking at the 2026 financial targets provided with the fourth quarter results, total revenues are expected to reach $26.00 billion at the midpoint, which would translate once again into 10%+ top-line growth. On that note, I would like to highlight how impressive it is to achieve double-digit growth on such a large revenue base. This is not what you would expect from a company supposedly facing an existential threat coming from artificial intelligence. Some might say that we are still too early in the game to see a tangible impact on Adobe's financial performance, but given where the stock is currently trading, a slowdown is already priced in. Otherwise, the stock would not be in the middle of a 60%+ drawdown.

Double-Digit Free Cash Flow Yield

In addition to a double-digit revenue growth, Adobe is currently trading at a free cash flow yield in excess of 10%. If I look into my past experience, I think it is quite rare for a company to combine double-digit top-line growth with a double-digit free cash flow yield. In my view, a share price below $260.00 created a highly asymmetric risk-reward ratio for those looking to buy Adobe's stock. As mentioned earlier, the stock is currently in the midst of a historical drawdown, and it is precisely when I like to dip my toes in a new story.

In fact, we only have to rewind back a few years to see that Adobe was widely considered as one of the highest quality compounders out there. When it was trading at its all-time high of almost $600.00 per share, Adobe was the epitome of 'Quality Investing'. Studying Trade Desk (TTD), ServiceNow, and Adobe over the last few days made me realize that quality investing is often synonymous with chasing a narrative. To be clear, I am the first to look for high-quality companies, but quality alone won't lead to a superior investment outcome. This is precisely why I am writing about Adobe today, as the fundamental quality is there, and this is combined with a highly compelling valuation.

As disclosed in Adobe's most recent quarterly results, the company generated operating cash flows of $3.2 billion in the fourth quarter of 2025. Given its capital-light business model, capital expenditures amounted to only $34 million over the same period, which translates into a quarterly free cash flow figure of $3.1 billion. On an annualized basis, and relative to ADBE's current market capitalization of $108.7 billion, this is equivalent to a free cash flow yield of ~11.5%.

With top-line growth expected to be once again into double-digit territory in fiscal 2026, free cash flows should grow further. But even if growth slows down into single-digit territory, it would not be the end of the world with an annualized free cash flow yield well in excess of 10% already. The significant multiple compression that occurred over the last few quarters indicates that growth expectations have cooled off quite a bit, which is the perfect setup for a new buyer like me.

Looking at Adobe's forward price-to-earnings ratio, we can see how it went from ~32.0x in early 2024 to ~11.0x as of right now. As mentioned above, Adobe expects non-GAAP earnings per share of $23.40 in fiscal 2026, which is how we end up with a forward price-to-earnings multiple of ~11.0x. However, if we use the GAAP earnings per share guidance of $18.00, the forward price-to-earnings ratio increases to ~14.4x. The key difference between GAAP and non-GAAP earnings related to share-based compensation, which is a substantial line item not only for Adobe, but for pretty much any SaaS company out there.

Risk Factor

If we go back to Adobe's cash flow statement for 2025, we can see how the company deployed $11.3 billion towards share repurchases. Looking at the income statement, it has translated into a 6% reduction in the number of diluted shares outstanding, from 443 million at the end of November 2024 to 417 million at the end of November 2025. While it is certainly good news to see a constant decline in ADBE's number of shares outstanding, I believe it is important to also include the significant share-based compensation expense in the picture. On one hand, Adobe is aggressively buying back shares, but on the other hand, the effect of those buybacks is in part offset by the issuance of shares to employees and executives. In fiscal 2025 alone, the company's share-based compensation amounted to $1.9 billion, leaving $9.4 billion worth of share repurchases on a net basis.

In my view, the topic of share-based compensation represents a risk not only for Adobe but for the software industry in general, and this is especially true in an environment of rapidly declining share prices. Given its non-cash nature, large share-based compensation expense can significantly overstate the profitability of a company when looking at cash-flow metrics. While non-GAAP financial metrics can highlight the financial performance of the underlying business, looking at GAAP metrics paints a more accurate portrait of Adobe's real profitability.

Another element that I would like to point out is the impact of share-based compensation on employees' morale. When a meaningful proportion of your remuneration comes from stock options, the behaviour of your employer's stock becomes particularly important. When the share price is in a strong uptrend, it works great for everyone. The value of the employees' holdings keeps increasing, and for the employer, it also limits dilution.

However, the opposite is also true when the share price is in the midst of a historic drawdown, as is the case with Adobe today. Employees can see and feel the declining value of their stock holdings, and to offer the same compensation in dollars, the employer must also issue more shares, translating into higher dilution for existing shareholders. This is the situation in which Adobe is finding itself in today, hence why I view share-based compensation as a risk.

Conclusion

Adobe is currently in the midst of one of its largest-ever share price drawdowns. Similar to many other software names I have previously covered, this is primarily due to fears related to AI disruption. In ADBE's particular case, I think those fears are now overstated, as I would expect the company's customer base to remain more sticky than what is currently implied by market prices.

Generally, Adobe Creative Cloud is deeply-imbedded in customers' workflow, and switching to a new offering is not as easy as it sounds. Moreover, artificial intelligence is now an integral part of Adobe's offering, including multiple new Photoshop functionalities, for example. In this context, I view Adobe as a key enabler for artificial intelligence instead of a target for disruption.

Based on the company's most recent guidance, top-line growth is expected to remain in double-digit territory in fiscal 2026. This is accompanied by a net cash balance sheet and an annualized free cash flow yield of ~11.5% excluding the impact of share-based compensation. Even if we include $2.0 billion in share-based compensation expense, the free cash flow yield remains substantial at approximately 9.8%.

How many times do we have the opportunity to buy a company with a double-digit free cash flow yield, a double-digit top-line growth and a net cash balance sheet? The 'Strong Buy' rating reflects my view that the current share price below $260.00 creates a highly asymmetric risk-reward ratio. Nobody knows the future and the ultimate impact artificial intelligence will have on our lives. However, what I do know is that the lower Adobe's share price goes, the higher the probability of success becomes. The best times to buy are often when it is the most difficult to do so.
